text: We are seeing a case of a sixty two years old male with a lower thoracic esophageal squamous cell carcinoma post neoadjuvant chemoradiation. The growth was starting at thirty five centimeters and there was a good response to neoadjuvant chemoradiation.
The thoracoscopy is performed with the patient in the prone position, utilizing ports placed in the mid axillary line and mid scapular line, providing access to the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon opens the mediastinal pleura to expose the azygos vein. Dissection is performed around the vein to encircle and ligate it, providing access to the esophagus.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the azegos vein, revealing clear tissue planes. The vein is hooked and ligated below where it enters the superior vena cava (SVC), then clipped above and divided in between, enhancing the exposure of the esophagus.
The surgeon identifies the bronchial artery located behind the esophagus, which arises from the intercostal artery.

The surgeon incises the mediastinal pleura anterior to the azygos vein, encountering and sharply dissecting numerous direct esophageal branches from the descending thoracic aorta. The dissection progresses from above below towards the carina and extends to the hiatus.
The surgeon uses pneumodissection to separate loose areolar tissue, revealing distinct anatomical layers as they are carefully dissected out.

The thoracic duct is visible as a yellow structure located just behind the azygos vein and above or posterior to the esophagus. Direct esophageal branches from the descending thoracic aorta are clearly identifiable.
The surgeon carefully dissects descending structures near the esophagus, ensuring not to open the left pleura by maintaining proximity to the esophagus during the procedure.

The surgeon incises the mediastinal pleura anterior to the esophagus, extending from the carina to the crura. The inferior pulmonary ligament is divided between the right lung and the esophagus, reaching the level of the inferior pulmonary vein.

The surgeon carefully lifts and separates the esophagus from the pericardium below, revealing the white caudal structure of the right vagus nerve running parallel. Care is taken to avoid dividing the nerve before it branches to the right bronchus.

The surgeon performs gradual dissection to create a circumferential plane around the esophagus, away from the tumor site. Umbilical tape is used to encircle the esophagus, providing traction for further dissection by allowing movement up and down.

The surgeon uses umbilical tape tied around the esophagus to provide traction, aiding in dissection. The descending thoracic aorta is visible, and branches from the aorta to the esophagus are carefully dissected down to the level of the crura.

The surgeon uses a hook to manipulate the esophagus, but larger vessels are addressed with harmonic or vessel sealers. The descending thoracic aorta is visible as a bulging structure above the esophagus.
The surgeon dissects below the level of the esophageal growth, revealing the gastroesophageal junction pad of fat and the circular brown diaphragmatic crura being carefully separated.

The surgeon carefully dissects the esophagus away from the left bronchus and trachea, with visible inflating and collapsing movements of these structures below. The dissection proceeds above the carina, using a Ligasure device to avoid injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve.

The surgeon takes down the bronchial artery arising from the intercostal artery for improved exposure. Loose fibers between the trachea and esophagus are sharply dissected using a ligature device. Slow dissection progresses towards the left side to prevent injury to the recurrent laryngeal nerve.
The surgeon carefully dissects the esophagus away from the left bronchus, taking care to avoid incising parallel structures such as the recurrent laryngeal nerve.

The surgeon completes dissection below the level of the diaphragmatic crura and proceeds to the thoracic inlet, marked by the inferior thyroid artery on both sides. Subcarinal lymphadenectomy is performed, with visible subcarinal lymph nodes near the carina. The whitish wall of the bronchus and red vascular structures are preserved using a vessel sealer to prevent injury.

The surgeon performs a total two-field lymphadenectomy, including dissection around the left recurrent laryngeal nerve. The vagus nerve is carefully pushed aside to preserve its branch to the left bronchus, completing the lymphadenectomy.
The surgeon identifies and dissects a lymph node at the level of the bronchus. The fibrillating atria are visible, indicating the patient's atrial fibrillation.
The surgeon shifts focus to the supracarinal region, carefully preserving branches to the trachea originating from the arch or descending aorta.

The surgeon identifies the superior pulmonary vein as a whitish structure below the surgical field, while dissecting in the aortopulmonary window. Above, the arch of the aorta is visible as a curving structure.

The surgeon carefully dissects parallel to the esophagus, using either blunt or sharp dissection with scissors. Short bursts of energy are applied using harmonic or ligature devices, avoiding the use of cautery to prevent injury to adjacent structures.

The surgeon sharply divides the branches of the left recurrent laryngeal nerve to the esophagus and pushes them away. The nerve is seen hooking around the arch of the aorta and running parallel to the trachea and esophagus. The inferior thyroid artery is identified as a white structure, and lymph nodes along this artery are dissected and removed. The left vagus nerve is observed hooking around the arch of the aorta, giving rise to the left recurrent laryngeal nerve.

The surgeon focuses on the right side of the trachea, carefully dissecting the mediastinal pleura away from the right vagus nerve. The right paratracheal lymph nodes are meticulously dissected using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, including scissors and short bursts from a vessel sealer. The lymph nodes are separated from the nerves and sharply divided, with care taken to avoid injuring nearby vascular structures.

The surgeon identifies the superior vena cava (SVC) below and pulls the mediastinal pleura to detach its attachment from the superior part of the esophagus. The right vagus nerve is visible as a whitish structure at the level of the right subclavian artery, where it gives rise to the right recurrent laryngeal nerve. The branches of the right recurrent laryngeal nerve to the esophagus are divided using a combination of sharp and blunt dissection. The right vagus and recurrent laryngeal nerve are denuded of lymphofatty tissues, completing the right-sided paratracheal lymphadenectomy. The right subclavian artery is also visible, and the traced structure is the right recurrent laryngeal nerve arising from the right vagus.
The surgeon completes the removal of lymph nodes from the surgical field. An intercostal drainage (ICD) tube is introduced, and the lung is visibly inflated under direct vision.

In the French position, laparoscopy is performed using standard upper abdomen ports. The left lobe of the liver is retracted with a tooth self-locking retractor. The surgeon dissects the gastrocolic ligament away from the arcade, progressing from the right gastroepiploic artery to the fundus.

The surgeon approaches the spleen, where the arcade ends and the short gastric vessels originate. Using a combination of posterior and lateral dissection, the short gastric vessels are carefully released, ensuring no injury to the vessels or the stomach due to the narrow space.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to carefully separate the fundus from surrounding tissues, minimizing the risk of lateral injury.

The surgeon pulls down the esophagus, performing dissection superiorly and towards the left side to separate it from the crura. The phrenoesophageal membrane is incised, and the mediastinum is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the tumor posteriorly, with some crural structures involved, and removes them. Attention is then directed to the right side, where the right crura is separated from the esophagus. The circumferential dissection of the gastroesophageal junction is completed, and small attachments on the right side within the gastrocolic ligament are also taken down.

The surgeon identifies the vertical structure as the right gastroepiploic artery, which is preserved. The omental attachments between the duodenum and colon are dissected, facilitating the movement of the gastric conduit towards the midline. The right gastrointestinal arcade is taken down, followed by posterior dissection after lymphadenectomy. The left gastric artery is ligated, and the gastric conduit is prepared using an open technique.
